text: HIGH COURT Windeyer, for the appellant. There is nothing in the evide to show that the petitioner in any way connived at the adultery or was guilty of such wilful neglect or misconduct as to condueets it; sees. 17 (b) and 19 (b) (iii.) of Act No. M4of 1899, The adultery had begun long before the acts of alleged connivance, and the wife had actually deserted the petitioner and refused to return to him, though the petitioner continued to support her and the children 'The watching for evidence of adultery under such circumstances is not evidence of connivance. 'The petitioner had endeavoured in every way to heal the breach between his wife and himself, and having failed in that, set about obtaining strict proof for the purposes of establishing his title to relief in the Court.
citation: Davis v Davis and Hughes [1904] HCA 40

text: He was as much entitled to watch his wife himself, as to employ a detective to do so. He cannot be said to have brought about his wife's fall. Short of absolute physical compulsion, he had done everything that was possible to save her.
citation: Davis v Davis and Hughes [1904] HCA 40

text: It is not necessary that he should act with the nicest discrimination, wisdom ot delicacy, so long as he acts with an honest intention, with a view to the assertion of his legal rights. 'The Court below relied on Gipps v. Gipps (1). 'The headnote to that case is misleading in suggesting that fora husband to merely abstain from interferingto prevent an adulterous intercourse, without any improper or corrupt intention on his part, is connivance. If taken literally, that is i stent with all the other leading cases on the subject.

text: They all _ establish the necessity for the presence of a corrupt intention in the mind of the husband. Moreover, in that case, the adultery had not begun at the time when the acts of alleged connivance took place, and interference might have prevented or delayed it But in the present ease it had begun, and the husband was pric: tically powerless to arrest it, his wife being out of his influence and control. Pring J.,does not appear to have regarded the case as an authority for the proposition contended for, because he assumes the necessity of a corrupt intention, but says that it may be inferred.
citation: Davis v Davis and Hughes [1904] HCA 40

Justice Walker dismising the appellant's petition for a divorce from his wife on ground of adultery. 'The facts of the case are not in dispute and are in a very small compass. [His Honor stated the facts as above, and proceeded.] On these facts the learned Judge of first instance apparently held that the petitioner had been guilty of conduct conducing to his wife's adultery. The petitioner appealed to the Full Cou who held that the petitioner had been guilty of connivance, the connivance relied on being watching to obtain evidence of # suspected adultery.
citation: Davis v Davis and Hughes [1904] HCA 40

text: Of course, in one sense, he might possibly have prevented the adultery as soon as he saw the co-respondent approaching the house, by taking steps to prevent him from entering it. There is a passage in the judgment of Lord Chelms 'ford in the ease of Gipps v. Gipps (1), relied on in the Supreme Court, which is very material on this point: "It must be bome in mind that the offence of adultery is complete in a single instance of guilty connection with a married woman.
citation: Davis v Davis and Hughes [1904] HCA 40

text: It is the first act which constitutes the crime, and though the adulterous intercourse between the parties should continue for years, there is not a fresh adultery upon every repetition of the guilty acts, although all and each of them may furnish proof of the adultery itself." Now in this case the husband suspectel the fact of adultery, and watched to obtain evidence for the purpose of proving it. The learned Judges of the Supreme Coutt thought that this amounted to connivance. I will state what we conceive to be the law as to connivance.
citation: Davis v Davis and Hughes [1904] HCA 40

text: As far as we know, there is no conflict of opinion on this point to be found in the books. The matrimonial law is derived from the Cano Law. The first case cited to us was Phillips v.

text: Sanchez was a great writer on the Canon Law, and probably the generally acecepted vie taken of connivance is derived from his work De Matrimonis The passage is in lib. 10 disp. 12, No. 52—I shall read in English—*It is lawful for a man who. suspeets his of adultery to watch her with proper witnesses so as to able to convict her 'of adultery, first because that is not oa niving at the offence but taking advantage of her wickedn for his own advantage ; secondly, because it is one thing invite, advise, or enjoin the commission of a w rong thing, whi is never lawful, and another to allow, or abstain from remo the opportunity for wrong-doing, which is sometimes perm for the sake of some greater good For i parents or masters of a household do no wrong in abstaining fre removing some opportunity for theft from their children or depe ants, when they know that they are addicted to it, in order that th may by such means be caught in the theft and recalled to rectitude" 'The analogy put by Sanchez shows that he did not think connivance to watch for the purpose of discovering the exis of a suspected fact, and it is manifestly in that sense Lord Stowell used the words that a man may let the licentio ness of his wife take its full scope, that is to say, if he sus her, for the purpose of. convicting her.
